for (i = 0 ; i < len; i+=2) name[i/2] = name[i];
  name[i/2]='\0';
finding.short_description: Heap buffer overflow via off-by-one when writing null terminator for odd-length input
finding.long_description: When `len` is odd (e.g., len=1), the loop exits with `i` equal to the next even number past `len`. The null terminator is then written at `name[i/2]`, which can be one byte past the allocated buffer. For example, with len=1: the buffer is 1 byte (index 0), the loop runs once (i=0, then i becomes 2), and `name[2/2] = name[1] = '\0'` writes one byte beyond the heap allocation. This is a heap-based off-by-one overflow that could corrupt heap metadata, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the actual bug fixed in this commit: cli_malloc(len) allocates insufficient space for the null terminator written by name[i/2]='\0', causing a one-byte heap overflow. The fix is exactly cli_malloc(len+1). While the CVE description references pe.c, this commit in sis.c was part of the ClamAV 0.97.8 security fixes associated with CVE-2013-2020, and the finding accurately describes the vulnerability present in the provided code.

actual_issue_summary: Off-by-one heap buffer overflow in getsistring() in sis.c: the buffer allocated with cli_malloc(len) is too small to hold the null terminator written at name[i/2] after the conversion loop, fixed by allocating len+1 bytes.

cve_desc: A vulnerability was found in ImageMagick, causing an outside the range of representable values of type 'unsigned long' at coders/pcl.c, when crafted or untrusted input is processed. This leads to a negative impact to application availability or other problems related to undefined behavior.

finding.short_description: Integer overflow in page dimension calculations from attacker-controlled PCL data
finding.long_description: The bounds values (bounds.x1, bounds.y1, bounds.x2, bounds.y2) are parsed via sscanf from the PCL file content, which is attacker-controlled. If bounds.x2 - bounds.x1 yields a negative double, casting to size_t (unsigned) results in a very large value. Similarly, the subsequent multiplication of page dimensions by resolution values can overflow or produce extremely large size_t values. These dimensions are then used to construct geometry strings passed to the delegate command, potentially causing denial of service through excessive memory allocation or other unexpected behavior downstream.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the same vulnerability as CVE-2022-32546: casting a double (from floor(bounds.x2-bounds.x1+0.5)) directly to size_t without range checking, which can produce values outside the representable range of unsigned long. The fix adds CastDoubleToLong() to safely handle this conversion, which is exactly what the diff shows.

actual_issue_summary: Direct cast of double to size_t (unsigned long) for width and height at coders/pcl.c without range validation. When crafted input produces out-of-range double values, the cast results in undefined behavior. The fix wraps the conversion with CastDoubleToLong() to safely clamp values.

cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a null pointer dereference vulnerability in the mpgviddmx_process function in reframe_mpgvid.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service. This vulnerability is possibly due to an incomplete fix for CVE-2021-40566.

finding.short_description: Bounds checks rely on assert() which is a no-op in release builds, enabling out-of-bounds access
finding.long_description: Multiple critical bounds checks are performed via assert() statements (e.g., `assert(remain>=size)`, `assert(remain>=current)`). In release/production builds where NDEBUG is defined, these assertions are compiled out entirely. If the parsed `size` from gf_m4v_parse_frame exceeds `remain`, or `current` exceeds `remain`, subsequent pointer arithmetic and memcpy operations will read or write out of bounds, leading to heap buffer overflows or over-reads. An attacker can craft a malicious MPEG video bitstream where the parser returns a frame size larger than remaining data to trigger this.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2021-40575) is a null pointer dereference (CWE-476) caused by insufficient remaining bytes (<5) when the code proceeds to parse start code headers. The fix adds a `remain<5` check before the header parsing section. The finding instead describes a general concern about assert() being compiled out in release builds, which is a different issue entirely and not what the CVE addresses.

actual_issue_summary: After dispatching remaining bytes in the loop, the code proceeds to parse start code headers without checking if enough bytes remain (at least 5). When remain < 5, operations like gf_bs_read_int or gf_bs_read_u8 on insufficient data lead to a null pointer dereference. The fix adds a `remain<5` check that stores leftover bytes and breaks out of the loop before attempting to parse headers.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. An attacker can craft a TFLite model that would cause an integer overflow in `TfLiteIntArrayCreate`. The `TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes` returns an `int` instead of a `size_t. An attacker can control model inputs such that `computed_size` overflows the size of `int` datatype. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Returned array data is uninitialized, potentially leaking sensitive heap data
finding.long_description: The function allocates memory with `malloc` and sets `ret->size`, but never initializes the `data` array elements. Callers that read from the array before writing to all elements will consume uninitialized heap memory, which could contain sensitive data from previous allocations. While this depends on caller behavior, defensive allocation with `calloc` or explicit zeroing would prevent information disclosure.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about an integer overflow in `TfLiteIntArrayCreate` where `alloc_size` is stored as `int` instead of `size_t`, allowing an attacker to cause an integer overflow leading to undersized allocation. The finding describes uninitialized memory, which is a completely different issue unrelated to the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: The variable `alloc_size` was declared as `int` instead of `size_t`, so when `TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes` returns a large value, it could overflow the `int` type, potentially resulting in a small or negative allocation size that bypasses the safety check, leading to a heap buffer overflow.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. TensorFlow's type inference can cause a heap out of bounds read as the bounds checking is done in a `DCHECK` (which is a no-op during production). An attacker can control the `input_idx` variable such that `ix` would be larger than the number of values in `node_t.args`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. This is the only affected version.

finding.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on protobuf repeated field guarded only by DCHECK (disabled in release builds)
finding.long_description: The index `ix` (derived from `edge->src_output()`) is validated against `node_t.args_size()` using `DCHECK`, which is compiled out in release/optimized builds. If `ix >= node_t.args_size()`, the call to `node_t.args(ix)` performs an unchecked access on the underlying protobuf `RepeatedPtrField`, resulting in an out-of-bounds read. An attacker who can craft a graph with mismatched output indices could trigger this, potentially reading arbitrary memory or causing a crash.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-23592: the DCHECK on `ix < node_t.args_size()` is a no-op in production, allowing an attacker-controlled `ix` to cause a heap out-of-bounds read via `node_t.args(ix)`. The fix replaces the DCHECK with a proper bounds check.

finding.short_description: Negative index from dst_input() not validated before use, DCHECK insufficient in release builds
finding.long_description: The value of `edge->dst_input()` is stored in an `int` and only validated via `DCHECK` (unsigned comparison). In release builds, a negative `dst_input()` value would pass silently. When passed to `std::vector::at()`, the negative int is implicitly converted to a very large `size_t`, causing an `std::out_of_range` exception (denial of service). Additionally, the DCHECK comparison `edge->dst_input() < input_nodes.size()` compares signed int with unsigned size_t, which can give incorrect results for negative values even in debug builds.

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a different potential issue with `dst_input()` validation that is not part of CVE-2022-23592. The CVE specifically concerns the `ix`/`input_idx` bounds check against `node_t.args_size()`. The `dst_input()` DCHECK was not changed in the fix and is a separate concern.

actual_issue_summary: The bounds check on `ix` (derived from `input_idx[i]`) against `node_t.args_size()` was done via DCHECK, which is disabled in production builds. This allowed an attacker to supply an `ix` value exceeding the number of args in the type definition, causing a heap out-of-bounds read when accessing `node_t.args(ix)`. The fix replaces the DCHECK with a proper runtime bounds check that logs a warning and returns early.

cve_desc: MP4Box is a component of GPAC-2.0.0, which is a widely-used third-party package on RPM Fusion. When MP4Box tries to parse a MP4 file, it calls the function `diST_box_read()` to read from video. In this function, it allocates a buffer `str` with fixed length. However, content read from `bs` is controllable by user, so is the length, which causes a buffer overflow.

finding.short_description: Stack buffer overflow when reading string into fixed-size buffer without bounds checking
finding.long_description: The function reads bytes one at a time from the bitstream into a stack-allocated buffer `str[1024]` without checking if `i` exceeds 1023. A crafted input with more than 1023 non-null bytes before a null terminator will overflow the buffer, allowing an attacker to overwrite adjacent stack memory including the return address, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the CVE-2022-1441 vulnerability: a fixed-size stack buffer of 1024 bytes is read into without bounds checking, and user-controllable input can exceed that size, causing a buffer overflow. The fix replaces this with a dynamically allocated buffer sized to s->size.

actual_issue_summary: A stack-based buffer overflow in diST_box_read() where user-controlled data from the bitstream is read byte-by-byte into a fixed 1024-byte stack buffer with no bounds check, allowing overflow when the input exceeds 1023 bytes.

cve_desc: A vulnerability was found in ImageMagick, causing an outside the range of representable values of type 'unsigned long' at coders/pcl.c, when crafted or untrusted input is processed. This leads to a negative impact to application availability or other problems related to undefined behavior.

finding.short_description: Heap buffer overflow writing to 'density' with MagickPathExtent size
finding.long_description: The 'density' buffer is allocated via AcquireString(""), which in ImageMagick 6 allocates a buffer of MaxTextExtent bytes. However, the subsequent FormatLocaleString call uses MagickPathExtent as the size limit, which is a different constant (typically from ImageMagick 7 and may be larger than MaxTextExtent). If MagickPathExtent > MaxTextExtent, this would write beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential buffer size mismatch unrelated to CVE-2022-32546. The CVE is about casting a double to size_t (unsigned long) producing out-of-range values in the width/height calculation. The diff only changes the double-to-size_t casts to use CastDoubleToLong().

finding.short_description: Integer overflow in page dimension calculation from attacker-controlled input
finding.long_description: The page width and height are computed by multiplying parsed bounding box dimensions (from the PCL input file) by image resolution and dividing by default resolution. Since bounds.x1, bounds.y1, bounds.x2, bounds.y2 are parsed from the input file via sscanf, an attacker can supply extremely large values. The multiplication of page.width/height by resolution values can produce very large floating point numbers, and the cast to size_t can result in integer overflow or unexpectedly large allocations.

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies the core issue: attacker-controlled double values from the input are cast to size_t without range checking, which can produce values outside the representable range of unsigned long. The fix adds CastDoubleToLong() to safely handle this conversion, matching CVE-2022-32546 exactly.

actual_issue_summary: In coders/pcl.c, the width and height calculations cast the result of floor() directly to size_t, which is undefined behavior when the double value (derived from attacker-controlled bounding box coordinates) is negative or outside the representable range of unsigned long. The fix uses CastDoubleToLong() to safely clamp the values.

cve_desc: GPAC 2.1-DEV-rev87-g053aae8-master. has a Null Pointer Dereference vulnerability in gf_isom_parse_movie_boxes_internal due to improper return value handling of GF_SKIP_BOX, which causes a Denial of Service. This vulnerability was fixed in commit 37592ad.

finding.short_description: Uninitialized variable totSize when GPAC_DISABLE_ISOM_FRAGMENTS is defined
finding.long_description: The variable `totSize` is declared but only initialized inside the `#ifndef GPAC_DISABLE_ISOM_FRAGMENTS` preprocessor block. When `GPAC_DISABLE_ISOM_FRAGMENTS` is defined, `totSize` is used uninitialized in multiple `totSize += a->size` statements throughout the function. While `totSize` does not appear to be used after the loop in a security-critical way, reading an uninitialized variable is undefined behavior in C and could lead to unpredictable results.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about a NULL pointer dereference on the box pointer `a` when `gf_isom_parse_root_box` returns GF_SKIP_BOX (a non-negative value), leaving `a` as NULL, which is then dereferenced in `switch (a->type)`. The fix adds `if (!a) return GF_ISOM_INVALID_FILE;`. The finding about uninitialized `totSize` is a completely different issue unrelated to the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: NULL pointer dereference when gf_isom_parse_root_box returns a non-negative status code (like GF_SKIP_BOX) without setting the box pointer `a`, which is then dereferenced in `switch (a->type)`. The fix adds a NULL check for `a` after verifying the return code is non-negative.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalAvgPoolGrad` does not consider cases where the input tensors are invalid allowing an attacker to read from outside of bounds of heap. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Division by zero when pooling cell has no elements
finding.long_description: If in_row_end < in_row_start or in_col_end < in_col_start (which can happen when pooling sequence values are crafted such that the clamped end is less than the start), num_elements_in_pooling_cell can be zero or negative. Although the inner loops wouldn't execute for zero-size cells, a case where num_elements_in_pooling_cell computes to zero through integer arithmetic (e.g., overflow) would cause a division by zero. More practically, if start > clamped end, the value is negative, and while the loops won't execute, this indicates missing validation.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2022-21730) is about out-of-bounds heap read (CWE-125) caused by negative values in row/col pooling sequence tensors being used as indices into the in_backprop matrix. The fix adds checks that these values must be non-negative. The finding describes a hypothetical division by zero (CWE-369), which is not the vulnerability that was fixed. While the finding tangentially notes 'missing validation,' it misidentifies the primary impact and vulnerability type.

actual_issue_summary: Negative values in row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor were not validated, allowing them to be used as indices (in_row_start, in_col_start, in_row_end, in_col_end) into heap-allocated matrices, causing out-of-bounds heap reads. The fix adds checks that these pooling sequence values are non-negative.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. When decoding a tensor from protobuf, a TensorFlow process can encounter cases where a `CHECK` assertion is invalidated based on user controlled arguments, if the tensors have an invalid `dtype` and 0 elements or an invalid shape. This allows attackers to cause denial of services in TensorFlow processes. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Tensor object left in partially-updated inconsistent state on certain code paths
finding.long_description: When N is 0 (a valid empty tensor) or when the condition (N > 0 && proto.dtype()) is false for a valid dtype (impossible after the DT_INVALID check, but the intent is unclear since proto.dtype() is used as a boolean), the pointer p remains nullptr and buf_ is set to nullptr while shape_ and dtype are updated from the potentially untrusted proto. More critically, if N is negative due to an integer overflow in num_elements(), the condition N > 0 fails, p stays nullptr, the old buf_ is unreffed, and the tensor is left with the new (malicious) shape but a null buffer. Subsequent code that trusts shape_.num_elements() to reflect the buffer size could read/write out of bounds or dereference null, leading to crashes or memory corruption.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the vulnerable code path: when N <= 0 (e.g., 0 elements or incomplete shape), the code skips the dtype validation in the if-block and proceeds directly to set_dtype(proto.dtype()) with a potentially invalid dtype. This is exactly the code path the CVE describes. The fix adds an else-branch to validate proto.dtype() in this case. While the finding attributes the wrong specific impact (memory corruption instead of CHECK/DCHECK assertion failure in set_dtype) and uses different CWEs, it correctly identifies the missing validation of proto.dtype() on the N<=0 path as the core problem.

actual_issue_summary: When decoding a tensor from protobuf with an invalid dtype and 0 elements (or incomplete shape), the code skips dtype validation (the `if (N > 0 && proto.dtype())` block) and calls `set_dtype(proto.dtype())` with the invalid dtype, triggering a DCHECK assertion failure and causing a denial of service crash. The fix adds an else-branch to validate proto.dtype() even when N <= 0.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `SparseCountSparseOutput` is vulnerable to a heap overflow. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Negative batch index is not validated, leading to out-of-bounds access on per_batch_counts.
finding.long_description: The variable `batch` is read from user-controlled sparse indices (int64_t) and cast to int, but only checked against `num_batches` (upper bound). A negative value for `indices_values(idx, 0)` will pass the `batch >= num_batches` check and be used to index into `per_batch_counts`, a vector of size `num_batches`. This results in out-of-bounds memory read/write, which can lead to denial of service or potentially arbitrary code execution.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE's PoC explicitly uses indices=[[-1, -1]] to trigger the heap overflow. The finding correctly identifies that negative batch indices bypass the upper-bound check and cause out-of-bounds access on per_batch_counts, which is the exact vulnerability.

finding.short_description: Integer truncation when casting int64_t shape dimension to int for num_batches.
finding.long_description: The `shape_vector(0)` value is of type `int64_t` but is assigned to an `int` variable `num_batches`. If the user supplies a shape with a first dimension larger than INT_MAX, the value silently truncates, potentially becoming negative or a small positive number. This corrupts the size of `per_batch_counts` and can cause subsequent out-of-bounds accesses when valid batch indices exceed the actual allocated size, leading to heap corruption or information disclosure.

judgments.reasoning: While this is a real potential issue, the CVE specifically describes a heap overflow caused by negative indices (as shown in the PoC: indices=[[-1,-1]]). The integer truncation of shape dimensions is a different issue from what this CVE addresses.

actual_issue_summary: Heap overflow in SparseCountSparseOutput caused by negative sparse indices (e.g., indices=[[-1,-1]]) being used as batch indices without proper lower-bound validation, allowing out-of-bounds writes to the per_batch_counts vector. The fix completely restructured the code to use splits-based batching instead of index-based batching, eliminating the attack vector.

cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

finding.short_description: Out-of-bounds read due to incorrect order of bounds check and memory access
finding.long_description: The while loop condition dereferences `bd->textstart + s - 1` before checking that `s > 0`. When `s` reaches 0, the expression `*(bd->textstart + s - 1)` reads one byte before `bd->textstart`, which is an out-of-bounds read. The check `s > 0` should be evaluated first (placed on the left side of `&&`) to short-circuit the memory access. This can be triggered when `exclude_trailing_space` is true and the text consists entirely of whitespace, or when `bd->textlen` is 0.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the exact bug fixed by the commit: the condition order in the while loop allows an out-of-bounds read before `bd->textstart`. The CVE is classified as CWE-787 (heap-based buffer overflow) by NVD, but the commit message says 'Reading before the start of the line' and the fix is swapping the order of conditions to check `s > 0` first. The finding's CWE-125 (out-of-bounds read) actually matches the commit description more precisely. This is the same underlying vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: In yank_copy_line(), the while loop condition checks VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + s - 1)) before verifying s > 0, causing an out-of-bounds read (reading before the start of the text buffer) when s becomes 0. The fix swaps the order so s > 0 is checked first, enabling short-circuit evaluation to prevent the invalid memory access.

cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in tildearrow Furnace. It has been classified as problematic. This is due to an incomplete fix of CVE-2022-1211. It is possible to initiate the attack remotely but it requires user interaction. The issue got fixed with the patch 0eb02422d5161767e9983bdaa5c429762d3477ce.

finding.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read on extFxColors array for effect values >= 0xe0
finding.long_description: When `pat->data[i][index]` is >= 0xe0, the code indexes into `extFxColors` with `pat->data[i][index] - 0xe0`, which can range from 0 to any value since there is no upper bound check on the data value. If the pattern data contains a value like 0xFF, the index would be 31; if it can exceed 0xFF or if extFxColors is smaller than 32 elements, this results in an out-of-bounds read. A maliciously crafted pattern file could trigger this to leak memory or cause a crash.

judgments.reasoning: The fix adds a bounds check `pat->data[i][index] > 0xff` before using the value to index into extFxColors. Without this check, values exceeding 0xff would produce a large index into extFxColors (and transitively into uiColors), causing an out-of-bounds read that leads to a crash (DoS). This matches the CVE description of a denial of service via a crafted file, and is an incomplete fix of CVE-2022-1211 which addressed similar array indexing issues.

actual_issue_summary: Pattern effect data values exceeding 0xFF were not bounds-checked before being used to index into the extFxColors array (via `pat->data[i][index] - 0xe0`), causing an out-of-bounds array access and crash. The fix adds a check for values > 0xFF, treating them as invalid effects instead of indexing into the array.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.ResourceScatterDiv` is vulnerable to a division by 0 error. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/resource_variable_ops.cc#L865) uses a common class for all binary operations but fails to treat the division by 0 case separately. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4aacb30888638da75023e6601149415b39763d76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Potential division by zero when N is zero in updates reshaping
finding.long_description: Although the outer `if (N > 0)` guard should prevent N from being zero at this point, the value of N is derived from `indices.NumElements()` cast to `Index` type. If `Index` is a signed type and `N_big` equals `std::numeric_limits<Index>::max() + 1` (due to the `<=` check rather than `<`), the cast could produce zero or a negative value depending on overflow behavior, potentially leading to division by zero in `num_updates / N`.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE is about division by zero in the scatter division operation itself (ResourceScatterDiv) when the updates tensor contains zero values. The fix validates that updates don't contain 0 when the op is DIV. This finding describes a hypothetical division by zero in `num_updates / N` due to integer overflow, which is a different issue entirely.

finding.short_description: Off-by-one in bounds check allows integer overflow when casting to Index
finding.long_description: The check uses `<=` instead of `<` when comparing N_big against `std::numeric_limits<Index>::max()`. If Index is a signed integer type (e.g., int32), N_big could equal 2^31 which, when cast to a signed 32-bit integer, overflows to a negative value or INT_MIN. This corrupted value of N then flows into scatter operations, potentially causing out-of-bounds memory access. The same issue applies to the `params->dim_size(0)` check.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE is about division by zero when ResourceScatterDiv receives updates containing zero values. This finding describes a potential integer overflow issue in the Index cast, which is unrelated to the CVE fix.

actual_issue_summary: ResourceScatterDiv does not validate that the updates tensor contains no zero values before performing division, leading to a floating-point exception (division by zero). The fix adds a check that rejects updates containing 0 when the operation is DIV on CPU devices.

cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.3 was discovered to contain a stack overflow in the function njs_default_module_loader at /src/njs/src/njs_module.c. NOTE: multiple third parties dispute this report, e.g., the behavior is only found in unreleased development code that was not part of the 0.7.2, 0.7.3, or 0.7.4 release

finding.short_description: Stack buffer overflow: length check does not account for info->name.length when dir is non-NULL
finding.long_description: When `dir` is non-NULL, the variable `length` is set to `dir->length` (plus an optional trailing slash), but `info->name.length` is not added to it. The subsequent bounds check `length > NJS_MAX_PATH` therefore only validates the directory portion. The function then copies both `dir` and `info->name` into the stack buffer `src[NJS_MAX_PATH + 1]`. If `dir->length` is small enough to pass the check but `dir->length + trail + info->name.length` exceeds `NJS_MAX_PATH + 1`, a stack-based buffer overflow occurs via the `njs_cpymem` of `info->name`. An attacker who controls the module name (e.g., a very long relative import path) can overwrite the return address or other stack data, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly identifies the bug: `length = dir->length` should be `length += dir->length`, causing the bounds check to miss the name length and leading to a stack buffer overflow. This matches the CVE and the fix.

actual_issue_summary: In njs_module_path(), `length = dir->length` overwrites the previously stored `info->name.length` instead of adding to it (`length += dir->length`). This causes the NJS_MAX_PATH bounds check to only consider the directory length, allowing a stack buffer overflow when both dir and name are copied into the fixed-size `src` buffer.

cve_desc: libjxl v0.3.7 is affected by a Divide By Zero in issue in lib/extras/codec_apng.cc jxl::DecodeImageAPNG(). When encoding a malicous APNG file using cjxl, an attacker can trigger a denial of service.

finding.short_description: Division by zero when delay_den is zero in fcTL chunk
finding.long_description: delay_den is read directly from the fcTL chunk data. A crafted APNG file can set delay_den to 0, which leads to a division by zero in delay_num * 1000 / delay_den. The APNG specification states that a value of 0 should be treated as 100, but this code does not implement that check, resulting in undefined behavior / crash.

judgments.reasoning: This exactly matches CVE-2021-36692: a divide by zero in DecodeImageAPNG when delay_den is 0, fixed by adding `if (!delay_den) delay_den = 100;`.

finding.short_description: Integer underflow in fdAT chunk size calculation leads to out-of-bounds read/write
finding.long_description: When processing fdAT chunks, the code computes chunk.size - 16 and chunk.size - 4. Since chunk.size is an unsigned int, if a crafted fdAT chunk has a size smaller than 16, chunk.size - 16 underflows to a very large value. This corrupted size is written via png_save_uint_32 and then a very large length is passed to processing_data, which can cause out-of-bounds memory reads when the PNG library processes the data.

judgments.reasoning: This describes a different potential issue (integer underflow in fdAT processing) unrelated to the CVE-2021-36692 divide-by-zero vulnerability. The fix only adds a check for delay_den being zero.

actual_issue_summary: Division by zero in DecodeImageAPNG when delay_den read from an fcTL chunk is 0. The fix sets delay_den to 100 (per APNG spec) when it is zero, preventing the division by zero in `delay_num * 1000 / delay_den`.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in the C language. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a denial-of-service vulnerability that affects PJSIP users that consume PJSIP's XML parsing in their apps. Users are advised to update. There are no known workarounds.

finding.short_description: Infinite loop possible when PI or comment construct is never properly terminated
finding.long_description: In the PI handling block, if the input contains '<?' but never '?>', pj_scan_get_until_ch will repeatedly fail or return empty results, and the loop may never break. Similarly the comment handling loop scanning for '>' can loop indefinitely on malformed input. This allows a denial-of-service attack with crafted unterminated constructs.

judgments.reasoning: The actual infinite loop vulnerability (CVE-2022-24763) is in the CDATA parsing section's while loop where pj_scan_strcmp checks for ']]>' but never advances past ']' if the exact sequence isn't found. The fix adds pj_scan_advance_n(scanner, 1, PJ_FALSE) in that CDATA loop. This finding incorrectly points to the PI ('<?') and comment ('<!') handling loops, which were NOT changed by the fix and are not the source of the vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: Infinite loop in CDATA XML parsing: the while loop checking for ']]>' terminator via pj_scan_strcmp would loop forever because after pj_scan_get_until_ch found a ']' that wasn't part of ']]>', the scanner position never advanced past it. The fix adds pj_scan_advance_n(scanner, 1, PJ_FALSE) before pj_scan_get_until_ch in the loop to ensure forward progress.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer has a use of unitialized variable. If the `train_nodes` vector (obtained from the saved model that gets optimized) does not contain a `Dequeue` node, then `dequeue_node` is left unitialized. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Use of uninitialized pointer `dequeue_node` when no dequeue op is found
finding.long_description: The local variable `dequeue_node` is declared without initialization. If no node in `train_nodes` satisfies `IsDequeueOp`, the pointer remains uninitialized. The subsequent `if (dequeue_node)` check reads an indeterminate value, which is undefined behavior. Even if that check happens to be skipped, the later loop `input_node->name() != dequeue_node->name()` unconditionally dereferences the uninitialized pointer, leading to a crash or potential code execution. An attacker who can influence the graph (e.g., by providing a graph with no dequeue ops) can trigger this.

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly identifies the CVE: `dequeue_node` is declared without initialization (`const NodeDef* dequeue_node;`), and the fix initializes it to `nullptr`. CWE-457 (Use of Uninitialized Variable) is a precise match for the NVD-listed CWE-703 (Improper Check or Handling of Exceptional Conditions), as the uninitialized variable is the root cause.

actual_issue_summary: The pointer `dequeue_node` was declared without initialization. If no Dequeue node exists in `train_nodes`, the pointer remains indeterminate, causing undefined behavior when checked in `if (dequeue_node)` and when dereferenced later. The fix initializes it to `nullptr`.

cve_desc: A memory leak flaw was found in the Linux kernel in the ccp_run_aes_gcm_cmd() function in drivers/crypto/ccp/ccp-ops.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption). This vulnerability is similar with the older CVE-2019-18808.

finding.short_description: Memory leak of 'tag' DMA workarea when ccp_set_dm_area fails
finding.long_description: In the decryption path, if `ccp_init_dm_workarea(&tag, ...)` succeeds but the subsequent `ccp_set_dm_area(&tag, ...)` fails, execution jumps to `e_tag`. However, `e_tag` does not call `ccp_dm_free(&tag)` — that call only happens on the successful path inside the else block. This means the DMA workarea allocated for `tag` is leaked. An attacker who can repeatedly trigger this error path could exhaust DMA memory resources.

judgments.reasoning: This is bug #3 from the commit message: the &tag was leaked on one error path. The fix adds `ccp_dm_free(&tag)` before the goto. This is one of the three resource leak bugs that constitute CVE-2021-3744.

actual_issue_summary: Multiple resource leaks in ccp_run_aes_gcm_cmd(): (1) if ccp_init_data(&src) fails, aad is not freed (goto e_ctx instead of e_aad); (2) the e_tag label should be e_final_wa — one error path after aes() fails skips freeing final_wa; (3) tag workarea is leaked when ccp_set_dm_area(&tag) fails because the goto skips ccp_dm_free(&tag).

cve_desc: ext/wddx/wddx.c in PHP before 5.6.25 and 7.x before 7.0.10 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an invalid wddxPacket XML document that is mishandled in a wddx_deserialize call, as demonstrated by a stray element inside a boolean element, leading to incorrect pop processing.

finding.short_description: Potential use-after-free due to freeing stack entry without nullifying the stack slot
finding.long_description: When ent1->data is IS_UNDEF, the code frees ent1 with efree() and decrements stack->top. However, the stack slot still contains the pointer to the freed memory. If subsequent parsing operations cause wddx_stack_top to return this same memory region (e.g., if stack->top is manipulated back), the freed memory could be accessed, leading to a use-after-free condition. This could potentially be exploited for code execution or information disclosure by carefully crafting malicious WDDX input that manipulates the stack state.

judgments.reasoning: The finding identifies the same bug as CVE-2016-7132. The vulnerable code calls efree(ent1) even when stack->top == 1 (the else branch), meaning the entry is freed but remains accessible on the stack since stack->top is not decremented. Subsequent operations (e.g., from stray elements inside a boolean element) access this freed memory, causing a NULL pointer dereference. The finding correctly identifies the root cause as use-after-free, which is the mechanism that leads to the NULL pointer dereference (CWE-476) described in the CVE. The fix moves efree(ent1) inside the stack->top > 1 branch only.

actual_issue_summary: When processing IS_UNDEF entries in wddx_deserialize, efree(ent1) was called unconditionally, including when stack->top == 1. This freed the entry while it remained accessible on the stack (since stack->top wasn't decremented in that branch), causing a NULL pointer dereference / use-after-free when subsequent stray XML elements triggered access to the freed stack entry.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: DCHECK is a no-op in release builds, allowing invalid input to proceed unchecked
finding.long_description: The assertion that input_requested_min_float < 0.0f when out_type_ is DT_QINT8 uses DCHECK, which is compiled away in non-debug (release/optimized) builds. In production, a non-negative input_requested_min_float with DT_QINT8 output type would bypass this check and could lead to incorrect quantization behavior or downstream issues. This should use OP_REQUIRES instead to enforce the constraint in all build configurations.

judgments.reasoning: This is one component of the incomplete validation described in CVE-2021-37665. The fix explicitly replaced the DCHECK with OP_REQUIRES to enforce the constraint in all builds. While the CVE also covers missing validation of input dimensions and scalar requirements, this finding correctly identifies part of the vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2021-37665 involves incomplete validation in MKL requantization ops. The fix reorders validation to occur before data access (checking input.dims()==4 before using input data), adds validation that input_requested_min/max are scalars, and replaces a debug-only DCHECK with a proper OP_REQUIRES check for the QINT8 min float constraint.

cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

finding.short_description: Write to potentially read-only internal buffer returned by ml_get()
finding.long_description: When depth == -1, 'line' points to the buffer returned by ml_get(), which is an internal memline buffer that should not be modified. The code modifies this buffer by writing NUL (p[i] = NUL) and then restoring the original character. This can corrupt Vim's internal buffer data structures. If intervening operations (like msg_outtrans_attr triggering autocommands or screen redraws) cause the memline to reallocate, the restore operation writes to freed/stale memory. This is both a data corruption and potential use-after-free issue.

judgments.reasoning: This describes a component of the same underlying issue: ml_get() returns a pointer to internal memory that can be invalidated. The fix copies the line to file_line via get_line_and_copy() to avoid exactly this class of use-after-free/stale pointer problems.

finding.short_description: Stale pointer dereference: 'startp' and 'line' may be invalidated after getfile()/ml_get() calls
finding.long_description: The variable 'startp' is set to point into 'line' (which may come from ml_get()). Later, when action is ACTION_GOTO or ACTION_SPLIT, the code calls getfile() or setpcmark() which can trigger autocommands and buffer switches that invalidate the pointer returned by ml_get(). After these calls, the expression 'startp - line' computes a column offset from potentially freed or reallocated memory. This could lead to setting the cursor to an incorrect or out-of-bounds column, or crash if the memory has been freed.

judgments.reasoning: This is the same use-after-free vulnerability (CVE-2022-1968). The line pointer from ml_get() can become stale/freed when other operations occur. The fix copies the line content to a local buffer to prevent this.

finding.short_description: Integer overflow in max_path_depth doubling may lead to undersized allocation
finding.long_description: Each time the file stack needs to grow, max_path_depth is doubled (max_path_depth *= 2). If this function is called with deeply nested includes, repeated doubling can cause max_path_depth to overflow a 32-bit int. While ALLOC_MULT may check for overflow in the multiplication with sizeof(SearchedFile), the separate 'max_path_depth * 2' computation in the argument could wrap around to a small or negative value first, leading to an undersized allocation. Subsequent array accesses using indices up to the old max_path_depth would then write out of bounds.

judgments.reasoning: This is unrelated to CVE-2022-1968. The CVE is about use-after-free from ml_get() returning internal pointers that get invalidated. This integer overflow finding describes a different, speculative issue.

actual_issue_summary: ml_get() returns a pointer to Vim's internal memline buffer which can be freed/invalidated by subsequent operations (autocommands, buffer switches, screen redraws). The fix replaces ml_get() calls with get_line_and_copy() which copies the line content into a local buffer (file_line), preventing use-after-free when the internal buffer is reallocated.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In versions prior to and including 2.12.1 a stack buffer overflow vulnerability affects PJSIP users that use STUN in their applications, either by: setting a STUN server in their account/media config in PJSUA/PJSUA2 level, or directly using `pjlib-util/stun_simple` API. A patch is available in commit 450baca which should be included in the next release. There are no known workarounds for this issue.

finding.short_description: Unbounded write to fixed-size msg->attr array leads to buffer overflow
finding.long_description: The while loop parses attributes and stores them in msg->attr[msg->attr_count], incrementing attr_count each iteration, but never checks whether attr_count has exceeded the size of the msg->attr array. A crafted STUN message with many small attributes could cause writes past the end of the array, leading to heap or stack corruption and potential remote code execution.

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly matches CVE-2022-31031: the unbounded loop writing into msg->attr without checking against the array size causes a stack buffer overflow. The fix adds the bounds check `msg->attr_count < attr_max_cnt`. CWE-787 matches as well.

actual_issue_summary: In pjstun_parse_msg, the while loop parsing STUN attributes writes into the fixed-size msg->attr array without checking that msg->attr_count stays within bounds, allowing a crafted STUN message with many attributes to overflow the array (stack buffer overflow). The fix adds a bounds check against the array size.

cve_desc: scheme/webauthn.c in Glewlwyd SSO server through 2.5.3 has a buffer overflow during FIDO2 signature validation in webauthn registration.

code_snippets: unsigned char data_signed[200], client_data_hash[32], cert_export[32], cert_export_b64[64];
finding.short_description: Stack-based buffer overflow when building data_signed from externally-controlled lengths
finding.long_description: The buffer data_signed is a fixed 200-byte stack buffer, but it is populated by concatenating rpid_hash (rpid_hash_len), client_data_hash (32), credential_id (credential_id_len), cert_x (cert_x_len), and cert_y (cert_y_len) plus 2 constant bytes. None of these lengths are validated before the memcpy calls. For example, credential_id_len alone can be up to 1023 bytes per the WebAuthn spec, and rpid_hash_len is typically 32 but is caller-controlled. An attacker who provides a large credential_id or manipulated inputs can overflow data_signed, corrupting adjacent stack variables, return addresses, and potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches the CVE: a fixed 200-byte stack buffer for data_signed is replaced in the fix with a dynamically allocated buffer sized to the actual input lengths, preventing the buffer overflow.

actual_issue_summary: The function used a fixed 200-byte stack buffer (data_signed[200]) to build signature verification data from variable-length inputs (rpid_hash, client_data_hash, credential_id, cert_x, cert_y). Since these lengths are attacker-controlled and can exceed 200 bytes in total, this causes a stack buffer overflow. The fix replaces the static buffer with a dynamically allocated buffer sized exactly to the sum of all input lengths.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

finding.short_description: Calling dim_size(0) on a scalar (0-dimensional) tensor causes out-of-bounds access.
finding.long_description: The code validates that `input_min_tensor.dims() == 0 || input_min_tensor.dims() == 1`, allowing scalar tensors. However, when `axis_ != -1`, the code unconditionally calls `input_min_tensor.dim_size(0)` and `input_max_tensor.dim_size(0)`. For a scalar tensor (dims() == 0), calling `dim_size(0)` is an out-of-bounds access on the shape's dimension array. An attacker can craft inputs where `axis_ > 0` and `input_min_tensor` is a scalar to trigger this issue, leading to a crash or information disclosure.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2022-29192) is about the `axis_ == -1` code path, where `.scalar<T>()` is called on input_min/input_max tensors that are 1-dimensional (not scalar), causing a CHECK-failure. The fix adds OP_REQUIRES checks that input_min and input_max are scalars when axis is -1. Finding 0 describes a different issue in the `axis_ != -1` branch involving dim_size(0) on scalar tensors, which is not what this CVE addresses.

actual_issue_summary: When axis==-1, the code calls .scalar<T>() on input_min_tensor and input_max_tensor without verifying they are actually scalars. A 1-dimensional tensor with dims()==1 passes the existing validation but causes a CHECK-failure when .scalar<T>() is called, enabling denial of service. The fix adds explicit IsScalar checks before the scalar access.

cve_desc: tinyexr 0.9.5 has a integer overflow over-write in tinyexr::DecodePixelData in tinyexr.h, related to OpenEXR code.

finding.short_description: Missing NULL check on calloc return value before dereferencing exr_image->tiles
finding.long_description: The calloc call for exr_image->tiles does not have its return value checked for NULL. If the allocation fails (e.g., due to a large num_tiles value derived from attacker-controlled offsets.size()), the subsequent loop immediately dereferences exr_image->tiles[tile_idx].images, leading to a NULL pointer dereference and crash. An attacker can craft a malicious EXR file with a large number of tile offsets to trigger this.

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a missing NULL check on calloc in the tiled code path. The CVE is about an integer overflow in the scanline code path due to an unchecked large line_no value. The fix adds a bounds check on line_no, not a NULL check on calloc.

finding.short_description: No upper-bound validation on line_no allows out-of-bounds write in DecodePixelData
finding.long_description: After adjusting line_no by subtracting data_window[1], only a lower-bound check (line_no < 0) is performed. There is no check that line_no + num_lines <= data_height. Since line_no comes directly from attacker-controlled file data, a crafted EXR file can supply a large line_no that passes the >= 0 check but causes DecodePixelData to write pixel data past the allocated image buffer boundaries, leading to heap buffer overflow and potentially arbitrary code execution.

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies the core issue: line_no from the file is not validated for excessively large values, which can cause an integer overflow and out-of-bounds write in DecodePixelData. The fix adds exactly this check (line_no > (2 << 20) || line_no < -(2 << 20)). This matches CVE-2020-19490's description of an integer overflow over-write in DecodePixelData.

actual_issue_summary: A crafted EXR file can supply a very large line_no value (e.g., 2^20 or greater) in the scanline data, which causes an integer overflow and subsequent out-of-bounds write in tinyexr::DecodePixelData. The fix adds a bounds check rejecting line_no values with absolute value greater than 2<<20.

cve_desc: Integer overflow in the VNC display driver in QEMU before 2.1.0 allows attachers to cause a denial of service (process crash) via a CLIENT_CUT_TEXT message, which triggers an infinite loop.

finding.short_description: Integer overflow in VNC_MSG_CLIENT_CUT_TEXT length calculation allows heap buffer over-read
finding.long_description: When handling VNC_MSG_CLIENT_CUT_TEXT, the code reads a uint32_t `dlen` from the client and computes `8 + dlen` as the required message length. If the client sends a `dlen` value close to UINT32_MAX (e.g., 0xFFFFFFF8 or larger), the expression `8 + dlen` wraps around to a small value due to unsigned integer overflow. The function then returns this small value as the required number of bytes. On the subsequent call, `len` will not equal 1 or 8, so the code falls through to `client_cut_text(vs, read_u32(data, 4), data + 8)` which receives the original huge `dlen` value but operates on a buffer that contains far fewer bytes. This can lead to a large out-of-bounds read (or potentially write depending on `client_cut_text` implementation), causing information disclosure or a crash.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the integer overflow in the CLIENT_CUT_TEXT message handling where `8 + dlen` can overflow. This is the same root cause as CVE-2015-5239, which describes an integer overflow in VNC CLIENT_CUT_TEXT handling leading to denial of service. The fix adds a size limit check (1MB max) before the `8 + dlen` computation, preventing the overflow. While the CVE description mentions an infinite loop as the consequence, the finding's description of the integer overflow mechanism is accurate and describes the same underlying vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow in VNC CLIENT_CUT_TEXT message handling where a malicious client can send a large dlen value (up to 2^32-1), causing `8 + dlen` to overflow. This leads to an infinite loop / denial of service and excessive memory allocation. The fix adds a 1MB size limit on the payload.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateEnsemble` can result in a use after free error if an attacker supplies specially crafted arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/resource_ops.cc#L55) uses a reference counted resource and decrements the refcount if the initialization fails, as it should. However, when the code was written, the resource was represented as a naked pointer but later refactoring has changed it to be a smart pointer. Thus, when the pointer leaves the scope, a subsequent `free`-ing of the resource occurs, but this fails to take into account that the refcount has already reached 0, thus the resource has been already freed. During this double-free process, members of the resource object are accessed for cleanup but they are invalid as the entire resource has been freed. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5ecec9c6fbdbc6be03295685190a45e7eee726ab. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.short_description: Double-free when InitFromSerialized fails: Unref() may delete the object while unique_ptr still owns it
finding.long_description: When InitFromSerialized fails, the code explicitly calls result->Unref() which, for a reference-counted object starting with a refcount of 1, will delete the object. However, 'result' is a std::unique_ptr that still owns the pointer. When OP_REQUIRES triggers a return (or when the unique_ptr goes out of scope), it will call delete on the already-freed pointer, causing a double-free. This can lead to heap corruption and potentially arbitrary code execution.

judgments.reasoning: The finding accurately describes the CVE. The CVE is about a use-after-free / double-free caused by calling Unref() on a resource managed by a unique_ptr without releasing ownership. The finding identifies the same root cause (Unref frees the object, then unique_ptr destructor frees it again). CWE-415 (Double Free) is closely related to the assigned CWE-416 (Use After Free) — the CVE description itself mentions both the double-free and the use-after-free during cleanup. The fix is exactly adding result.release() after Unref() to prevent the double-free.

actual_issue_summary: In BoostedTreesCreateEnsemble, when InitFromSerialized fails, result->Unref() frees the resource but the std::unique_ptr still owns the pointer. When the unique_ptr goes out of scope, it attempts to delete the already-freed object, causing a use-after-free/double-free. The fix adds result.release() after Unref() to relinquish unique_ptr ownership.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.SparseReshape` can be made to trigger an integral division by 0 exception. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L176-L181) calls the reshaping functor whenever there is at least an index in the input but does not check that shape of the input or the target shape have both a non-zero number of elements. The [reshape functor](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L40-L78) blindly divides by the dimensions of the target shape. Hence, if this is not checked, code will result in a division by 0. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4923de56ec94fff7770df259ab7f2288a74feb41. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1 as this is the other affected version.

finding.short_description: Integer overflow in validation check `product * missing == dense_size`
finding.long_description: Even if `product` itself doesn't overflow, the multiplication `product * missing` used in the validation check can overflow for large values. If this overflows to equal `dense_size`, the check passes incorrectly, allowing an invalid reshape. This means the output shape dimensions would be inconsistent with the actual number of dense elements, which can lead to buffer over-reads or over-writes when the reshaped sparse tensor indices are computed by ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.FractionalAvgPoolGrad` can be tricked into accessing data outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/fractional_avg_pool_op.cc#L205) does not validate that the input tensor is non-empty. Thus, code constructs an empty `EigenDoubleMatrixMap` and then accesses this buffer with indices that are outside of the empty area. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 0f931751fb20f565c4e94aa6df58d54a003cdb30. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

The fix adds OP_REQUIRES checks that in_batch, in_rows, in_cols, and in_depth are all non-zero.

cve_desc: Puma is a HTTP 1.1 server for Ruby/Rack applications. Prior to versions 5.5.1 and 4.3.9, using `puma` with a proxy which forwards HTTP header values which contain the LF character could allow HTTP request smugggling. A client could smuggle a request through a proxy, causing the proxy to send a response back to another unknown client. The only proxy which has this behavior, as far as the Puma team is aware of, is Apache Traffic Server. If the proxy uses persistent connections and the client adds another request in via HTTP pipelining, the proxy may mistake it as the first request's body. Puma, however, would see it as two requests, and when processing the second request, send back a response that the proxy does not expect. If the proxy has reused the persistent connection to Puma to send another request for a different client, the second response from the first client will be sent to the second client. This vulnerability was patched in Puma 5.5.1 and 4.3.9. As a workaround, do not use Apache Traffic Server with `puma`.

finding.short_description: Safety-critical bounds checks rely on assert() which is disabled in release builds
finding.long_description: All bounds validation—including that `off <= len`, that pointers don't overflow, that `nread <= len`, `body_start <= len`, `mark < len`, and `field_start < len`—is performed exclusively via `assert()` macros. When compiled with `-DNDEBUG` (standard for release/production builds), all these checks are removed. If `off > len`, then `p = buffer + off` points past the end of the buffer while `pe = buffer + len` points before `p`, leading to out-of-bounds reads or pointer arithmetic wrapping. An attacker supplying crafted input could exploit the absence of these checks to read out-of-bounds memory.

The fix restricts header value characters to printable ASCII (33-126), tab (9), space (32), and CR (13), rejecting LF and other control characters. The finding about assert() checks being disabled in release builds is an entirely different concern unrelated to the HTTP request smuggling vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: The HTTP parser accepted any character (including LF/newline) in HTTP header field values. The fix restricts allowed characters in header values to printable ASCII (33-126), horizontal tab (9), space (32), and CR (13), rejecting LF (10) and other control characters. This prevents HTTP request smuggling when Puma is behind a proxy that forwards header values containing LF characters.
